title: What a Picture of a Dog Has to Do with Reason and Logic
gist: Estonian philosophers Uku Tooming and Roomet Jakapi argue in Neuropsychologia that people with aphantasia — who cannot form mental images at all yet reason fine with abstract concepts — undermine David Hume's centuries-old claim that abstract thought requires mental pictures.

insights.claim: 18th-century Scottish philosopher David Hume argued that abstract thought requires mental images — that one cannot consider the category 'dog' or 'triangle' without picturing a specific instance — and this Humean view persists in parts of philosophy today despite centuries of challenge.
insights.speaker: Kristen French
insights.so_what: A long-standing philosophical framework about the mechanics of abstract cognition, useful context for debates on how AI systems versus humans represent abstract concepts.
insights.locator: In the 1700s, Scottish philosopher David Hume argued

insights.claim: Estonian philosophers Uku Tooming and Roomet Jakapi of the University of Tartu, in a paper published in Neuropsychologia, argue that aphantasia — the inability to form mental images — completely undermines Hume's claim that abstract thought requires mental pictures, and they address the potential counter-arguments one by one.
insights.speaker: Kristen French
insights.so_what: A direct empirical/philosophical rebuttal of a foundational theory of mind, relevant to research on how abstract reasoning can be dissociated from mental imagery.
insights.locator: The latest challenge comes from Estonian philosophers

insights.claim: Aphantasia — a condition in which people cannot form mental images at all — affects only a tiny fraction of the population and was named by neurologist Adam Zeman in 2015, borrowing from Aristotle's concept of 'phantasia' (the imagination faculty that produces images).
insights.speaker: Kristen French
insights.so_what: A well-defined, named clinical population (aphantasia) offers a natural experiment for testing theories of abstract cognition without imagery.
insights.locator: These individuals, who represent a tiny fraction of the population

insights.claim: People with aphantasia draw a complete blank when asked to visualize a dog, tree, or bus — no fur, leaves, or wheels — yet they typically have no problem handling abstract concepts such as justice or algebra.
insights.speaker: Kristen French
insights.so_what: Empirical evidence that abstract reasoning can operate independently of mental imagery, the core fact grounding the philosophical rebuttal of Hume.
insights.locator: If asked to visualize a dog, a tree, or a bus
